name: CQRS & Event Sourcing

useCases.use: High-throughput order management systems requiring independent scaling of order intake (writes) and order status queries (reads)
useCases.dontUse: Simple CRUD applications with low write volume and simple query requirements

useCases.use: Systems requiring full audit trail and point-in-time reconstruction of domain state (financial, compliance, supply chain)
useCases.dontUse: Systems where eventual consistency is unacceptable and all reads must see the latest write immediately

useCases.use: Event-driven microservices architectures where domain events must be shared reliably across bounded contexts
useCases.dontUse: Legacy monolithic systems where introducing an event store creates disproportionate complexity

components: Azure Event Hubs — event store and event streaming backbone (Kafka-compatible)
Azure Service Bus — command queue for reliable command delivery to command handlers
Azure Cosmos DB — read model store (denormalised, query-optimised projections)
Azure SQL Database / PostgreSQL — write model store for transactional command processing
Azure Functions — event projectors (subscribe to events and update read models)
Azure Cache for Redis — read model caching layer for high-frequency queries
Azure Application Insights — command/query performance and event processing lag monitoring

guardrails.rule: Downstream consumers must be designed for eventual consistency — SLAs must define maximum acceptable projection lag
Events must be immutable, versioned, and backwards-compatible — breaking schema changes require a new event type
Events containing PII or Confidential data must be encrypted at the field level before publication to the event store
Projection lag must be monitored and alerting configured for lag exceeding defined SLA thresholds
Event store retention must align with business audit requirements — minimum 7 years for financial events

revisionHistory.author: Priya Sharma
revisionHistory.summary: Initial CQRS pattern based on order management platform pilot

revisionHistory.author: Priya Sharma
revisionHistory.summary: Added Event Sourcing guidance, PII field-level encryption guardrail, and Redis caching layer

strategicIntent: Separate read and write workloads using Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) and capture all state changes as an immutable event log using Event Sourcing — enabling independent scaling of read/write paths, complete audit history, and temporal queries for high-throughput transactional systems.
exceptionProcess: Submit Architecture Review Board request with justification for shared read/write model. Requires Integration Architecture Board sign-off and demonstration that projection lag is not acceptable for the use case.
interactionSteps: Client submits a command (e.g., PlaceOrder) to the Command API via Azure Service Bus queue
Command handler validates the command against business rules using the write model (Azure SQL/PostgreSQL)
Command handler persists the state change as an immutable domain event (e.g., OrderPlaced) to Azure Event Hubs (event store)
Event projectors (Azure Functions) subscribe to the event stream and update read model projections in Cosmos DB
Client queries the read model API backed by Cosmos DB (or Redis cache) for low-latency, denormalised views
For temporal queries or audit, the full event history is replayed from Event Hubs to reconstruct state at any point in time
Application Insights tracks command processing latency, event processing lag, and projection freshness SLAs
problemStatement: Single-model architectures that share the same data model for reads and writes suffer from impedance mismatch: read-optimised queries conflict with write-optimised transactional models, creating lock contention, scalability ceilings, and inability to reconstruct historical state for audit or debugging purposes.
securityConsiderations: Event store must be immutable — no delete or update operations allowed on committed events; use Event Hubs capture to ADLS for long-term retention
Command and query APIs must enforce Azure AD authentication with separate RBAC roles for writers and readers
Events containing PII must be encrypted at the event level using Azure Key Vault-managed keys before publication
Read model projections must not aggregate data across classification boundaries without explicit data classification review
Idempotency keys must be enforced on all commands to prevent duplicate processing in at-least-once delivery scenarios
